claim.hypotheses_location: Introduction: paragraph beginning 'We hypothesized that mortality salience should produce divergent cultural responses.' (page header shows 1020).
claim.statement_location: Abstract and General Discussion: Abstract states 'Mortality salience promoted culturally divergent responses, leading European Americans to defend the self and Asian Americans to defend other people.' (page header shows 1020); General Discussion reiterates this claim (pages 1022-1023).

data.source: Laboratory experiments with human participants (college student participants recruited for two experimental studies).
data.wave_or_subset: Study 1 and Study 2 (two separate experimental samples reported in the manuscript).
data.sample_size: Study 1: 22 European Americans and 20 Asian Americans (total N = 42). Study 2: 48 European Americans and 23 Asian Americans (total N = 71).
data.notes: Measures used include PANAS (delay/mood measure), Prostitution Attitudes Scale (PAS; α = .74) in Study 1, Assignment of Blame Scale (ABS; α = .62) in Study 2. Demographics reported include ethnicity, gender, age, and generation status for Asian Americans (first vs. second generation). No public dataset or repository link provided.

method.description: Two experiments using a mortality-salience manipulation (writing about one's own death) versus a control (writing about dental pain), followed by a filler/delay task (PANAS) and dependent measures assessing attitudes toward a nonnormative target (Study 1: fabricated legal brief about a prostitute; measures: Prostitution Attitudes Scale and assigned bail amount) or attributions about an innocent victim (Study 2: fabricated news article about a car accident; measure: Assignment of Blame Scale). Participants were European American and Asian American college students; analyses involved 2 (condition: mortality salience vs control) × 2 (ethnicity) ANOVAs and planned contrasts.
steps: Recruit college-student participants and classify by self-reported ethnicity (European American vs Asian American).
Randomly assign participants to mortality-salience condition (write thoughts about own death) or control condition (write about dental pain).
Administer PANAS as a delay task.

Study 1: Present a fabricated legal brief about a prostitute; collect assigned bail amount and administer the Prostitution Attitudes Scale (PAS). Study 2: Present a fabricated news article about an innocent victim; administer the Assignment of Blame Scale (ABS).
Compute net positive-mood scores from PANAS to examine mood effects.
Analyze data using 2 (condition) × 2 (ethnicity) multivariate/univariate ANOVAs, followed by planned t-test contrasts. Report internal consistencies for scales.
method.models: Multivariate ANOVA (2 × 2: condition × ethnicity) for Study 1 (Wilks's λ reported), univariate ANOVAs on each dependent measure, planned t-test contrasts; univariate ANOVA for Study 2 with planned contrasts. F-tests, t-tests reported.
method.outcome_variable: Study 1: Prostitution Attitudes Scale (PAS score), Assigned bail amount. Study 2: Assignment of Blame Scale (ABS score).
method.independent_variables: Experimental condition (mortality salience vs control) and participant ethnicity (European American vs Asian American).
method.control_variables: Mood (PANAS net positive-mood scores examined), gender tested (no significant effects reported).

results.summary: Across two studies, mortality salience produced culturally divergent processes: European Americans responded to mortality salience with increased self-protective responses (greater derogation/blame of dissimilar others), whereas Asian Americans responded with increased affirmation/closer responses to others (decreased derogation/blame). In Study 1, there was a significant condition × ethnicity multivariate interaction (Wilks's λ = .77, F(2,37) = 5.31, p = .009); PAS score showed a significant interaction (F(1,38) = 10.263, p = .003). In Study 2, there was a significant interaction on ABS scores (F(1,68) = 11.05, p = .001). Mood (PANAS) did not account for effects; gender showed no significant effects.

numerical_results.direction: More negative attitudes toward prostitution in mortality-salience condition (relative decrease in PAS score)
More favorable attitudes toward prostitution in mortality-salience condition (increase in PAS score)
Interaction indicating divergent effects of condition by ethnicity on PAS
Marginally higher bail in mortality-salience condition
Non-significant pattern opposite to European Americans (lower bail in mortality condition)
Marginal interaction between condition and ethnicity on bail amount
Increased blame of the victim in mortality-salience condition
Decreased blame of the victim in mortality-salience condition
Significant interaction showing opposite effects of mortality salience on blame by ethnicity

numerical_results.value: F(1,68) = 7.13 (Asian Americans assigned higher blame overall: M = 2.01, SD = 0.16 vs European Americans M = 1.50, SD = 0.13)
numerical_results.direction: Asian Americans assigned higher blame overall across conditions

metadata.original_paper_title: Culturally Divergent Responses to Mortality Salience
